AUTHORIZATION.title: Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA / IDOR)
AUTHORIZATION.description: API endpoints expose resources by ID but fail to validate if the current authenticated user has permission to access that specific object.
AUTHORIZATION.impact: Attackers can manipulate resource IDs in API requests (e.g. GET /api/orders/101 vs GET /api/orders/102) to read, update or delete private data belonging to other accounts.
remediation_steps: Implement a server-side ownership validation check before loading resources.
Never trust the resource ID provided in the HTTP request without querying permissions.
Use random, non-sequential UUIDs (v4) instead of auto-incrementing integer IDs to make resource harvesting difficult.

AUTHENTICATION.title: Broken User Authentication
AUTHENTICATION.description: API routes or endpoints are exposed without proper authentication checks or fail to validate security tokens (JWT) correctly.
AUTHENTICATION.impact: Unauthenticated or malicious clients can invoke private endpoints directly to retrieve sensitive lists or invoke business logic without logs or authorization.
remediation_steps: Secure all internal API endpoints using standard authentication middleware (e.g., JWT Validator).
Verify JWT signatures on the backend server, and assert expiration (exp) and scopes (scp).
Disable debugging routes and sandbox endpoints in production configurations.

RATE_LIMITING.title: Lack of Resources & Rate Limiting
RATE_LIMITING.description: API fails to restrict the number of requests a client can execute in a short period.
RATE_LIMITING.impact: Clients can execute brute-force attacks or trigger high-volume queries, leading to denial of service (DoS), server resource exhaustion, or high cloud billing.
remediation_steps: Implement client rate-limiting rules based on IP address, API key, or JWT identity.
Define standard rate limits (e.g., 60 requests per minute) on sensitive endpoints.
